meta.title: Election Setup Guide | Public Digit
meta.description: Learn how to configure every election setting on Public Digit — IP restrictions, ballot options, voter verification, and more.

page.title: Election Settings Guide
page.subtitle: A complete walkthrough of every setting available for your election — what it does, when to use it, and how to configure it correctly.

section_overview.heading: Overview
section_overview.body: Election settings let you precisely control how voters interact with your ballot. They are split into three areas: Voter Access Control (who can vote and from where), Ballot Options (how candidates are selected), and Voter Verification (identity checks before voting). You can change settings at any time before voting opens. Once the election is active and has received votes, a confirmation checkbox is required to acknowledge the impact of mid-election changes.
section_overview.tip: Settings are stored per election — changing them for one election has no effect on others in your organisation.

section_statemachine.heading: Election Lifecycle
section_statemachine.intro: Every election progresses through five distinct phases, each with its own rules and capabilities. The phase your election is in is determined automatically by the dates you set.
section_statemachine.key_concept: Elections are date-driven, not action-driven. You specify when voting begins and ends, and the system automatically manages phase transitions.

timeline.description: Your election follows this five-phase lifecycle:

phases.name: Administration
phases.what_happens: You create posts (positions), add candidates, configure voters, and customise all settings. No voters can access the ballot yet.

phases.name: Nomination
phases.what_happens: The administration phase has been locked in. Candidates review their information. You can still make adjustments, but the voting dates are now visible to all.
actions: Allow candidates to view their listings
Make final adjustments to posts and candidates

phases.name: Voting
phases.what_happens: The voting period is now open. Voters see the ballot and can submit their selections. The system locks voting immediately when this phase starts to ensure fair count.
actions: Voters access and complete the ballot
Monitor voting progress and real-time tallies
phases.note: Once voting opens, settings changes require explicit confirmation. Changing IP restrictions or verification rules can affect voters who are in progress.

phases.name: Results Pending
phases.what_happens: Voting has closed. The ballot is locked and no new votes are accepted. Results are being tabulated and prepared for publication.
actions: Review vote count and validate results

phases.name: Results
phases.what_happens: Final results are published. Voters can verify their own votes using their code. This is the final state — the election is complete.

dates_section.heading: How Dates Drive Phase Transitions
dates_section.description: Two critical dates determine when your election automatically progresses:

dates.when: Election automatically transitions from Nomination → Voting
dates.sets: The beginning of the voting period
dates.important: Voting is locked immediately when this phase starts to prevent tampering

dates.when: Election automatically transitions from Voting → Results Pending
dates.important: No votes are accepted after this moment. You must wait until results are counted.

dates_section.example: If you set voting_starts_at = 'Mar 1, 9am' and voting_ends_at = 'Mar 3, 5pm', the election will automatically move through Nomination → Voting (Mar 1 9am) → Results Pending (Mar 3 5pm).

permissions_section.heading: What Can Happen in Each Phase
permissions_section.description: Different actions are available depending on the current phase:

tips.label: Set dates strategically
tips.text: Leave enough time in Administration and Nomination phases. Once you set voting dates, they determine when the election automatically progresses.

tips.label: Voting is locked
tips.text: When voting begins, the system immediately prevents any further candidate changes. This ensures vote integrity.

tips.label: Dates are in server time
tips.text: All dates use the server's timezone. Make sure to account for timezone differences when setting voting start and end times.

section_prerequisites.heading: Before You Begin
section_prerequisites.intro: Make sure these steps are complete before configuring settings:

steps.title: Election created
steps.body: Your election must exist in the system with a name, dates, and at least one post (position).

steps.title: Voters registered
steps.body: Members must be imported as voters via the Voters tab. Settings have no effect until people are eligible to vote.

steps.title: Election not yet open
steps.body: Certain settings (especially IP restrictions and verification mode) are best configured before the election opens. Changing them after voting begins requires confirmation.

section_access.heading: Voter Access Control
section_access.intro: These settings control where and how often voters can cast their ballot.

ip_restriction.heading: IP Address Restriction
ip_restriction.body: When enabled, the system counts how many votes have been submitted from each IP address and blocks additional votes once the limit is reached. This prevents one computer from being used to submit votes on behalf of multiple people.
ip_restriction.analogy: Think of an IP address like a physical ballot box location. If your rule is 'each table may only submit 4 ballots', enabling IP restriction with max=4 enforces exactly that limit.
ip_restriction.toggle_body: Off by default. Turn on when your election is conducted at a shared location (office, community centre) where multiple voters share an internet connection.

max_per_ip.heading: Maximum Votes per IP Address
max_per_ip.body: Sets how many individual votes may be submitted from the same IP address. The default is 4.

table.scenario: Home voting (each voter on own connection)
Community hall with shared Wi-Fi (20 voters)

whitelist.heading: IP Whitelist
whitelist.body: Whitelisted IP addresses are exempt from the vote-per-IP limit. Use this for trusted locations — for example, your organisation's office server — that legitimately submit many requests.
formats: CIDR range (subnet): 192.168.1.0/24 means all addresses from 192.168.1.0 to 192.168.1.255
One address or range per line
whitelist.cidr_tip: CIDR (Classless Inter-Domain Routing) is a compact way to specify a range of IP addresses. The number after the slash (/24, /16) controls the range size. /32 = single IP. /24 = 256 addresses. /16 = 65,536 addresses. When in doubt, use a single IP address instead.

section_ballot.heading: Ballot Options
section_ballot.intro: These settings control what voters see on the ballot and how many candidates they must choose.

no_vote.heading: Allow "No Vote" / Abstain
no_vote.body: When enabled, voters see an additional option on each post (position) that lets them explicitly abstain — registering their participation without choosing any candidate.
no_vote.when: Enable this when your organisation's rules allow formal abstentions and you want them counted separately from 'did not vote'.
no_vote.label_tip: You can customise the text shown to voters. Common choices: "No vote", "Abstain", "None of the above", "Enthalten".

selection_constraint.heading: Selection Constraint Type
selection_constraint.body: Controls how many candidates a voter must or may select for each position. Choose the rule that matches your election bylaws.

table.description: Voters can select as many or as few candidates as they wish — including none.
Voters must select exactly the specified number. Submitting with more or fewer is blocked.
Voters must select at least the minimum and no more than the maximum.
Voters must select a minimum number but may select more.
table.example: Mandatory selection of at least 1, free to select more

table.description: Voters may select any number up to the maximum, including none.

selection_constraint.note: The constraint applies per post (position). If your election has multiple posts, each post is evaluated independently.

section_verification.heading: Voter Verification
section_verification.intro: Voter Verification adds a layer of identity confirmation before a voter is allowed to access the ballot. An administrator verifies each voter's identity (typically via a video call) and records their IP address and/or device fingerprint. When the voter arrives to vote, the system checks they match what was recorded.

modes.label: None (default)
modes.description: No per-voter identity check. Voters access the ballot using only their login credentials. IP restrictions from the Voter Access Control section still apply if enabled.

modes.label: IP Address Only
modes.description: The voter must vote from the same IP address that was recorded during their identity check. Useful for elections where the voter's physical location is predictable.

modes.label: Device Fingerprint Only
modes.description: The voter must use the same device (browser + hardware) that was verified. Works even if the voter changes location and their IP address changes.

modes.label: Both — IP + Device
modes.description: The highest security level. Voter must match both the recorded IP address and the same device. Recommended for high-stakes elections where identity fraud is a concern.

workflow_steps: Open the Voters tab for the election.
For each voter, click the Verify button to open the verification panel.
Conduct a video or audio call with the voter to confirm their identity.
While they are on the call, note their current IP address (they can visit whatismyip.com) and/or capture their device fingerprint.
Enter these details in the verification panel and save.
The voter's status changes to Verified. They can now access the ballot.
section_verification.bypass_note: Verified voters automatically bypass the per-IP vote count limit from the Voter Access Control section. This prevents a verified voter from being incorrectly blocked if they happen to share an IP with another voter.

section_scenarios.heading: Common Scenarios
section_scenarios.intro: Here are three typical election configurations to guide your setup.

scenarios.title: Simple Club Election
scenarios.description: Members vote online from their own homes over several days.
scenarios.rationale: Each voter is at home on their own connection. There is no shared-IP concern. No identity verification is needed beyond the platform login.

scenarios.title: Secure Board Election
scenarios.description: Board members vote in person from a single office. Only verified members may vote.
scenarios.rationale: All voters share the office internet connection. IP restriction limits total submissions. Voter verification ensures that only pre-identified individuals can vote.

scenarios.title: Diaspora Organisation
scenarios.description: Members vote remotely from different countries. Identity is checked beforehand via video call.
scenarios.rationale: Voters are geographically dispersed so IP restriction is unhelpful. Video-call verification with both IP and device fingerprint provides the highest assurance that the correct person is voting.

items.question: Can I change settings after the election has started?
items.answer: Yes. However, if the election is active and votes have been received, you will be required to check a confirmation box acknowledging that the change takes effect immediately. Some changes — like tightening IP restrictions — could block voters who have already started the process.

items.question: What happens if a verified voter's IP address changes?
items.answer: If Voter Verification mode is set to 'IP Only' or 'Both' and the voter's IP changes (e.g. they switched from Wi-Fi to mobile data), they will be blocked from voting. The administrator can update the verification record with the new IP address, or temporarily change the mode to 'None'.

items.question: What is a device fingerprint?
items.answer: A device fingerprint is a digital profile built from the voter's browser settings, screen resolution, installed fonts, and other technical signals. It identifies a specific browser on a specific computer without using cookies or storing personal data.

items.question: Does IP restriction affect verified voters?
items.answer: No. Verified voters bypass the IP count limit automatically. This prevents a legitimate voter from being blocked because they share an IP address with another voter.

items.question: What does the Settings History section show?
items.answer: It shows the current settings version number, when settings were last saved, and which administrator made the last change. This provides a basic audit trail for accountability.

items.question: Can I use different constraints for different posts in the same election?
items.answer: The selection constraint type is set at the election level and applies to all posts equally. If your election needs different rules per post, use the required number field on each post to control the exact count independently.
